concepts.title: Real Numbers and Their Types
concepts.definition: Real numbers include all rational and irrational numbers. Rational numbers can be written as p/q with integers p, q and q ≠ 0; irrational numbers cannot be expressed as such fractions.
examples: 3, −5, 2/7 and 0.25 are rational numbers.
√2 and π are irrational numbers.

concepts.title: Representation on Number Line and Decimal Expansions
concepts.definition: Real numbers can be placed on a number line. Rational numbers have terminating or recurring decimal expansions; irrational numbers have non‑terminating, non‑repeating decimals.
examples: 1/4 = 0.25 (terminating); 1/3 = 0.333… (repeating).
√2 ≈ 1.414213… is non‑terminating and non‑repeating.

concepts.title: Irrational Numbers and Their Properties
concepts.definition: Irrational numbers cannot be expressed as p/q; between any two distinct real numbers there are infinitely many rationals and irrationals.
examples: √5 and √7 are irrational.
(1 + √5)/2 is irrational.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents for Real Numbers
concepts.definition: Exponents of real numbers follow laws similar to integer exponents but extended to rational exponents as well.
formulae: a^(1/n) is an nth root of a

concepts.title: Definition and Degree of a Polynomial
concepts.definition: A polynomial in one variable x is an expression of the form a_n x^n + ... + a_1 x + a_0, where coefficients a_i are real and n is a non‑negative integer. Degree is the highest power of x with non‑zero coefficient.
examples: 3x^2 − 5x + 1 is a quadratic polynomial of degree 2.
7 is a constant polynomial of degree 0.

concepts.title: Types of Polynomials
concepts.definition: Polynomials are classified by degree (constant, linear, quadratic, cubic) and by number of terms (monomial, binomial, trinomial).
examples: 5x is linear monomial; x^2 + 2x is quadratic binomial; x^3 − x + 1 is cubic trinomial.

concepts.title: Zeroes of a Polynomial
concepts.definition: A zero (root) of polynomial p(x) is a value a such that p(a) = 0. A linear polynomial has at most one zero, quadratic two, etc.
examples: For p(x) = x − 3, x = 3 is the zero.
For q(x) = x^2 − 4, zeros are x = 2 and x = −2.

concepts.title: Remainder Theorem
concepts.definition: If a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), then the remainder is p(a).
examples: Dividing x^2 + 1 by (x − 1): remainder is p(1) = 1^2 + 1 = 2.

concepts.title: Factor Theorem and Factorisation
concepts.definition: If p(a) = 0 for polynomial p(x), then (x − a) is a factor of p(x). Factorisation expresses a polynomial as product of simpler polynomials.
examples: For p(x) = x^2 − 5x + 6, p(2) = 0, so (x − 2) is a factor; p(x) = (x − 2)(x − 3).

concepts.title: Cartesian Plane
concepts.definition: The Cartesian plane consists of two perpendicular number lines, x‑axis (horizontal) and y‑axis (vertical), intersecting at origin (0,0).
examples: Point (3, −2) is 3 units right of origin and 2 units below x‑axis.

concepts.title: Coordinates of a Point
concepts.definition: Ordered pair (x, y) gives the coordinates of a point: x‑coordinate (abscissa) and y‑coordinate (ordinate).
examples: In (−4, 5), −4 is abscissa, 5 is ordinate.

concepts.title: Quadrants
concepts.definition: The plane is divided into four quadrants by axes, with sign patterns of coordinates: (+,+), (−,+), (−,−), (+,−).
examples: (2, 3) lies in first quadrant; (−4, −1) in third quadrant.

concepts.title: Linear Equation in Two Variables
concepts.definition: An equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real and not both a and b zero, is a linear equation in two variables x and y.
examples: 2x + 3y − 6 = 0 is linear; xy + 2 = 0 is not.

concepts.title: Solution and Graph of a Linear Equation
concepts.definition: A solution is any ordered pair (x, y) satisfying the equation. The set of all solutions forms a straight line when plotted on Cartesian plane.
examples: For x + y = 4, (0,4), (1,3), (2,2) are solutions and lie on the same line.

concepts.title: Finding Solutions
concepts.definition: Solutions can be found by choosing a value for one variable and solving for the other.
examples: For 3x − y = 5, taking x = 2 gives 6 − y = 5 ⇒ y = 1, so (2,1) is a solution.

concepts.title: Axioms and Postulates
concepts.definition: Euclid’s geometry is based on axioms (self‑evident truths) and postulates (statements accepted without proof specific to geometry).
examples: Axiom: Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.
Postulate: A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.

concepts.title: Definitions and Basic Terms
concepts.definition: Basic terms like point, line, plane, line segment, ray, angle, circle are defined ideal objects in geometry.
examples: A line has length but no thickness and extends indefinitely; a plane surface extends infinitely in all directions.

concepts.title: Equivalent Versions in Modern Geometry (Concept)
concepts.definition: Euclid’s statements translate into modern language of points and sets; parallel postulate leads to uniqueness of parallels through a point.
examples: Through a point not on a line, exactly one line can be drawn parallel to the given line (Euclid’s fifth postulate equivalent).

concepts.title: Adjacent, Linear Pair and Vertically Opposite Angles
concepts.definition: Adjacent angles share a common arm and vertex; a linear pair is adjacent angles whose non‑common arms form a straight line; vertically opposite angles are non‑adjacent angles formed by two intersecting lines.
examples: If a straight line makes angles of 120° and x°, they form linear pair, so x = 60°.

concepts.title: Parallel Lines and Transversals
concepts.definition: When a line (transversal) cuts two lines, it forms various angle pairs: corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior and interior on same side.
formulae: If lines are parallel: corresponding angles equal; alternate interior angles equal; interior angles on same side supplementary
examples: If a transversal cuts parallel lines and one corresponding angle is 70°, all corresponding and alternate interior angles as per rules.

concepts.title: Angle Sum Property of a Triangle (Intro)
concepts.definition: Sum of interior angles of a triangle is 180°, often used with line and angle properties.
examples: If two angles of triangle are 50° and 60°, third angle = 70°.

concepts.title: Congruence of Triangles and Criteria
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if their corresponding sides and angles are equal; criteria include SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS and RHS for right triangles.
examples: If three sides of one triangle are equal to three sides of another, they are congruent by SSS criterion.

concepts.title: Properties of Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles
concepts.definition: In an isosceles triangle, angles opposite equal sides are equal; in an equilateral triangle, all angles are 60°.
examples: In triangle with sides 5, 5, 8, the base angles opposite sides 5 are equal.

concepts.title: Inequalities in a Triangle (Intro)
concepts.definition: In any triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than the third, and the larger angle is opposite the longer side.
examples: If sides are 3, 4, 5, then 5 is largest side, so angle opposite 5 is largest.

concepts.title: General Properties of Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: A quadrilateral has four sides and four angles, and the sum of interior angles is 360°.
examples: If three angles are 80°, 100° and 90°, fourth angle = 360° − 270° = 90°.

concepts.title: Parallelogram and Its Properties
concepts.definition: In a parallelogram, opposite sides are parallel and equal, opposite angles equal and diagonals bisect each other.
examples: If one pair of opposite sides in quadrilateral is both equal and parallel, quadrilateral is a parallelogram.

concepts.title: Special Parallelograms: Rectangle, Rhombus, Square
concepts.definition: Rectangles have all angles 90°; rhombuses have all sides equal; squares have all sides equal and all angles 90°, combining properties.
examples: Rectangle: diagonals equal and bisect each other.
Rhombus: diagonals bisect at right angles.

concepts.title: Area of Parallelogram
concepts.definition: Area of a parallelogram equals product of its base and corresponding height.
examples: Parallelogram with base 8 cm and height 5 cm has area 40 cm².

concepts.title: Area of Triangle
concepts.definition: Area of triangle is half the product of its base and corresponding height.
examples: Triangle with base 6 cm and height 4 cm has area 12 cm².

concepts.title: Triangles on the Same Base and Between Same Parallels
concepts.definition: Triangles on same base and between same parallels have equal area.
examples: Two different triangles on segment AB and between given parallels have equal area.

concepts.title: Basic Terms: Chord, Arc, Diameter, Sector, Segment
concepts.definition: Chord is line segment joining two points on circle; diameter is chord through centre; arc is part of circumference; sector is region between two radii and arc; segment is region between chord and arc.
examples: Semi‑circle is a sector with angle 180°.
Minor arc corresponds to minor segment and minor sector.

concepts.title: Angle Subtended by Chord and Equal Chords
concepts.definition: Equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the centre; conversely equal angles at the centre stand on equal chords.
examples: If two chords have equal central angles, their lengths are equal.

concepts.title: Perpendicular from Centre to Chord
concepts.definition: Perpendicular from centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord, and conversely if a radius passes through midpoint of a chord, it is perpendicular to the chord.
examples: For chord AB, if OC ⟂ AB at C, then AC = CB.

concepts.title: Basic Constructions
concepts.definition: Using ruler and compass to construct line segments, perpendiculars and angle bisectors following standard steps.
examples: Constructing perpendicular bisector of a given line segment.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles
concepts.definition: Triangles can be constructed given suitable data like SSS, SAS, ASA using compass and straightedge.
examples: Construct triangle ABC given AB, BC and AC (SSS construction).

concepts.title: Constructions Using Given Angles
concepts.definition: Angles of specific measures are constructed and used to form triangles and other figures.
examples: Constructing a triangle when two angles and the included side are known.

concepts.title: Semi-Perimeter of Triangle
concepts.definition: Semi‑perimeter is half of the sum of the three sides of a triangle.
examples: For triangle with sides 3, 4, 5, s = (3 + 4 + 5)/2 = 6.

concepts.title: Heron’s Formula for Area of Triangle
concepts.definition: Heron’s formula gives area of a triangle in terms of its three sides and semi‑perimeter.
examples: For triangle with sides 3, 4, 5, s = 6 and area = √[6 × 3 × 2 × 1] = √36 = 6 square units.

concepts.title: Applications to Quadrilaterals (Split into Triangles)
concepts.definition: Heron’s formula can be applied to quadrilaterals by dividing them into two triangles and adding their areas.
examples: Finding area of a plot shaped like irregular quadrilateral by drawing a diagonal and using Heron’s formula on both triangles.

concepts.title: Surface Area of Cube and Cuboid
concepts.definition: Surface area is sum of areas of all faces of a solid; for cube and cuboid there are direct formulas.
examples: Cube of side 4 cm has surface area 6 × 16 = 96 cm².

concepts.title: Surface Area of Right Circular Cylinder and Cone
concepts.definition: Curved and total surface areas of cylinders and cones depend on radius, height and slant height.
examples: Cylinder with r = 3 cm, h = 7 cm: curved area = 2π × 3 × 7 = 42π cm².

concepts.title: Volumes of Cube, Cuboid and Cylinder
concepts.definition: Volume is measure of space occupied; formulas depend on base area and height.
examples: Cuboid 5 × 3 × 2 cm has volume 30 cm³.

concepts.title: Collection and Organisation of Data
concepts.definition: Statistics deals with collection, organisation and interpretation of data in frequency tables and grouped forms.
examples: Recording marks of students and arranging into a frequency distribution.

concepts.title: Mean, Median and Mode of Ungrouped Data
concepts.definition: Mean is arithmetic average, median is middle value in ordered data, mode is most frequent value.
examples: For data 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, mean = 6; median = 6; if 4 occurs most, mode = 4.

concepts.title: Measures of Central Tendency (Basic Interpretation)
concepts.definition: Mean, median and mode summarise data using a single representative value, each with different uses and sensitivities to extreme values.
examples: Mean affected by very high or low values; median more robust to outliers.

concepts.title: Random Experiment and Sample Space
concepts.definition: A random experiment is an action whose outcomes cannot be predicted with certainty but all possible outcomes are known; set of all outcomes is sample space.
examples: Tossing a coin has sample space {H, T}; rolling a die has {1,2,3,4,5,6}.

concepts.title: Event and Favourable Outcomes
concepts.definition: An event is a subset of sample space. Favourable outcomes are those outcomes that belong to the event in question.
examples: Event ‘getting even number’ when rolling a die has favourable outcomes {2,4,6}.

concepts.title: Empirical Probability (Experimental)
concepts.definition: Empirical probability of an event is estimated from experiments as the ratio of number of times the event occurs to total number of trials.
formulae: P(E) ≈ (number of times E occurs) / (total number of trials)
examples: If a coin is tossed 100 times and shows head 55 times, empirical probability of head is 55/100 = 0.55.
